intro: Checkout.com and Stark Bank both show up under the payment gateway category, but they solve slightly different jobs. Checkout.com publishes an entry price of custom pricing; Stark Bank does not publish one. Both sit at moderate lock-in. Transparency lands at 4/5 versus 3/5. Checkout.com fits teams working on SaaS with online payments, while Stark Bank is a closer match when the job is Pix payment integration for Brazilian businesses via SDK. Worth noting: Checkout.com is explicitly not for offline, Internal projects; Stark Bank is explicitly not for companies outside Brazil or needing multi-country payment rails. The honest trade-off: Checkout.com trades off on offline-only retail; Stark Bank trades off on brazil-only — no utility outside BRL. On the plus side, Checkout.com highlights SaaS with online payments, while Stark Bank points to SDKs in 10+ languages — real DX focus. Checkout.com's documentation also calls out e-commerce checkout. Stark Bank similarly notes native Pix integration for instant BRL payments.
quick_take: Checkout.com is for SaaS with online payments; Stark Bank is for Pix payment integration for Brazilian businesses; decide on entry price.
verdict_a: Choose Checkout.com if your project is SaaS with online payments, usage-based billing at custom pricing matches your volume, medium lock-in is an acceptable trade-off.
verdict_b: Choose Stark Bank if your project is Pix payment integration for Brazilian businesses via SDK, medium lock-in is an acceptable trade-off.

faq.q: Is Checkout.com cheaper than Stark Bank?
faq.a: Checkout.com uses usage-based from custom pricing, and Stark Bank uses usage-based pricing. Checkout.com publishes an entry price of custom pricing, and Stark Bank does not, so a side-by-side cost comparison isn't possible from public data.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Checkout.com to Stark Bank?
faq.a: Our data puts Checkout.com at medium lock-in, and Stark Bank at medium lock-in (brazil-focused pix, sdks help portability). Migration is feasible but not trivial — budget time for re-integration, data export, and parallel running before cutover.
Both score 5/5 on developer experience in our data, so there's no clear winner on that axis. Checkout.com does edge ahead on pricing/docs transparency (4/5 vs 3/5), which can make evaluation faster.

faq.q: Is Stark Bank a good alternative to Checkout.com?
faq.a: Stark Bank is a reasonable alternative to Checkout.com when your workload leans more toward brazilian fintechs and SaaS needing Pix, TED, and boleto with clean SDKs than SaaS, E-commerce, Marketplace. One caveat: Stark Bank is explicitly not for companies outside Brazil or needing multi-country payment rails, so check that constraint against your use-case before switching.
